meta_title: For Students: SaaS pricing to Solve can't finish projects (Intermediate Idea List)
meta_description: Learn how to SaaS pricing and overcome can't finish projects. A Intermediate-level Idea List using Antigravity and Claude Code.

content.seo_title: For Students: SaaS Pricing to Solve Can't Finish Projects
content.seo_description: Stop stalling on your SaaS projects. Learn how implementing a structured pricing model can force scope clarity and help you ship your first real-world app.
content.answer_block: The root cause of unfinished projects is feature creep disguised as 'perfecting' the app. By defining your SaaS pricing model early, you force a hard constraint on your scope. This shift turns a vague student experiment into a market-ready product, providing the psychological closure needed to finally ship your code.
content.intro:
The graveyard of developer potential is paved with 'perfect' projects that never saw the light of day. You start with a vision, code for two weeks, and then drift into the abyss of endless feature iteration. You aren't failing because you lack skill; you are failing because you lack a business boundary.

Price is the ultimate filter for product scope. When you force yourself to define what a user pays for, you stop building 'everything' and start building 'the core value.' This guide will show you how to use pricing as a design architectural tool to force completion, ensuring your portfolio grows with finished, deployable SaaS apps.

steps.title: Define the Minimum Viable Value
steps.description: Identify the one feature your user will pay for. By focusing on a single paid tier, you eliminate the temptation to build secondary, non-essential features.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Analyze my core features list and identify the single most valuable component that justifies a paid subscription. Create a lean scope document that excludes all non-monetizable features.
steps.claude_code_command: claude run --task 'Generate a lean roadmap based on the core paid feature identified in antigravity'

steps.title: Architect the Subscription Logic
steps.description: Build the backend logic that restricts access. This forces you to understand user authentication and role-based access control (RBAC) early.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Create a database schema for a subscription-based SaaS. Include tables for users, plans, and active_subscriptions. Ensure the logic allows for feature-gating based on plan type.
steps.claude_code_command: claude add --file schema.sql --context 'Implement row-level security for subscription tiers'

steps.title: Integrate Payment Infrastructure
steps.description: Connect Stripe or LemonSqueezy. Implementing actual checkout flows provides the strongest psychological milestone for finishing a project.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Write a boilerplate for a Stripe Checkout session handler in Node.js. Include success and cancel URL redirects and a webhook listener for payment verification.
steps.claude_code_command: claude terminal --command 'npm install stripe && node setup-stripe.js'

steps.title: Build the 'Paywall' UI
steps.description: Create the friction points where users are prompted to upgrade. This ensures your UI is functional and navigation is logical.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Design a React component for a pricing table with three tiers: Free, Pro, and Enterprise. Add visual 'call to action' buttons that trigger the checkout flow.
steps.claude_code_command: claude edit --file pricing-table.tsx --prompt 'Add responsive styling and loading states for the upgrade buttons'

steps.title: Automate Deployment
steps.description: Finalize your build pipeline. Finishing a project means it is live on the internet, not just sitting in your local development environment.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Generate a Vercel/Railway deployment configuration file. Include environment variables for API keys and database connection strings.
steps.claude_code_command: claude run --task 'Configure automated CI/CD pipeline for production deployment'

pro_tips: Never build an 'Enterprise' tier until you have 10 paying customers; it is just a distraction from your core feature.
Use 'Feature Gating' as a development roadmap: if a feature isn't behind a paywall, it shouldn't be in your MVP.
Pricing is a marketing tool, not just revenue. Use a 'Free' tier to get users, but make the 'Pro' tier the only way to save data.
If you find yourself stuck, reduce the price to zero for the first 5 users—this removes the pressure of perfection and lets you focus on shipping.
Always build the 'Cancel Subscription' flow before you build the 'Upgrade' flow; it forces you to handle data cleanup and user experience properly.
content.next_steps: Once your app is live, focus on getting your first five users. Use their feedback to refine the pricing model, then iterate on the product. Do not add new features—polish the existing ones until they are bulletproof.
content.conversion_block: Stop letting your brilliant ideas rot in local folders. You have the tools, the roadmap, and the psychological framework to ship a real product that users pay for. Ready to build? Join our AI Builder community → Start your first project today.
